Name: Chegg Inc
Description: Online education platform offering textbook rentals, homework help, tutoring, and study tools to college and high school students. Peaked at ~$14.7B market cap during COVID remote-learning boom.
Impact: The most direct AI casualty in public markets. ChatGPT and other free AI tools have replaced Chegg's core homework help product, causing subscriber losses of 30%+ YoY. Stock has fallen 99% from its 2021 peak. Non-subscriber traffic dropped 49% after Google AI Overviews launched. Company has cut 45% of workforce and sued Google over AI search summaries.

Name: TTEC Holdings Inc
Description: Global customer experience (CX) outsourcing firm operating call centers and contact centers across 22 countries with ~50,000 employees. Provides outsourced customer care, technical support, and digital CX consulting.
Impact: AI chatbots and virtual agents are directly replacing the human contact center agents that form TTEC's core business model. Stock has fallen ~95% from its pandemic high. Clients are hesitant to commit spending due to AI uncertainty, and the per-seat billing model is structurally threatened by AI automation. Revenue declining ~5% annually with high debt load (5.5x market cap).

Name: Getty Images Holdings Inc
Description: Global visual content marketplace providing stock photos, editorial images, videos, and music to businesses and media. Library of 700M+ files. Announced $3.7B merger with Shutterstock in Jan 2025.
Impact: AI image generators (Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, Adobe Firefly) directly displace demand for generic stock photography. Getty's Creative segment declined ~5% YoY in 2024. Billions of AI images generated monthly now compete with stock libraries. Getty has tried to pivot by licensing its data to AI companies and building its own AI generator, but the core business of selling individual photo licenses is eroding.

Name: Ziprecruiter Inc
Description: Online employment marketplace connecting job seekers with employers through AI-powered matching technology. Two-sided platform with 4M+ businesses and 180M+ job seekers. Revenue peaked at $905M in 2022.
Impact: AI threatens both sides of the recruiting marketplace: it can automate candidate screening, resume matching, and initial outreach that employers currently pay platforms to do. Revenue has declined from $905M (2022) to $449M (2025). While macro hiring slowdown is the primary driver, AI-driven hiring tools are compressing the value of job board intermediaries and reducing the number of human roles companies need to fill.

Name: Trueblue Inc
Description: Staffing and workforce solutions provider offering temporary labor, on-demand staffing, recruitment process outsourcing, and managed services across construction, manufacturing, and transportation. Operates PeopleReady, PeopleScout, and PeopleManagement brands.
Impact: AI and automation threaten the temporary staffing model from two directions: AI-powered hiring platforms reduce need for staffing intermediaries, and automation/robotics reduce demand for the blue-collar temp labor that is TrueBlue's core business. Revenue declined 8% YoY in Q1 2025 and 22% in Q4 2024. The company faces structural headwinds as warehouse automation, self-checkout, and AI-driven logistics reduce demand for temporary workers.

Name: RWS Holdings plc
Description: UK-based provider of language services, translation, localization, and intellectual property (patent) services. One of the world's largest translation companies with operations in 100+ languages. Market-leading Trados translation software.
Impact: AI-powered machine translation (DeepL, Google Translate, ChatGPT) has commoditized basic translation work that was RWS's bread-and-butter. Stock down 57% YoY. Adjusted profit fell 43% in FY2025. Took £88M goodwill impairment on Language Services division. Price pressure from clients expecting AI-level efficiency at lower cost. The company is attempting a pivot to 'AI-enabled content solutions' but margins are compressing during the transition.

Name: Concentrix Corp
Description: Global technology-enabled business process outsourcing (BPO) company providing customer experience solutions, digital engineering, and technology services. ~300,000 employees across 40+ countries. Merged with Webhelp in 2023.
Impact: AI-powered chatbots, virtual agents, and automated customer service solutions threaten the labor-intensive customer support outsourcing model. Enterprise clients are increasingly deploying AI to handle routine inquiries that human agents currently process. Revenue growth has slowed significantly, and the market is pricing in structural displacement of call center labor. The company's massive workforce is its biggest asset and its biggest liability in an AI world.

Name: ManpowerGroup Inc
Description: Global workforce solutions and staffing company providing temporary and permanent recruitment, talent management, and workforce consulting across 75+ countries. One of the world's largest staffing firms.
Impact: AI automates recruiting workflows (screening, matching, scheduling) that staffing firms charge for, compressing margins. AI-driven workforce analytics reduce employer reliance on staffing intermediaries. Longer-term, AI and automation reduce overall demand for the white-collar temporary staffing that represents a significant portion of revenue. Revenue has been flat-to-declining as clients leverage internal AI tools for hiring.

Name: Robert Half Inc
Description: Staffing and consulting firm specializing in accounting, finance, technology, legal, and administrative professional placements. Also operates Protiviti consulting subsidiary. Focused on white-collar professional staffing.
Impact: AI directly threatens Robert Half's core business of placing white-collar professionals. AI coding tools reduce demand for contract programmers; AI accounting tools reduce demand for temp finance staff; AI legal research tools reduce demand for contract lawyers. Revenue has declined significantly from post-COVID peaks. The professional staffing segment is among the most exposed to AI displacement because the tasks are largely knowledge-based and automatable.

Name: Shutterstock Inc
Description: Global marketplace for licensed stock photographs, vectors, illustrations, videos, and music. Library of 900M+ images. Being acquired by Getty Images in $3.7B deal announced Jan 2025.
Impact: Same dynamic as Getty: AI image generators (DALL-E, Midjourney, Adobe Firefly) directly replace demand for stock photos, especially for generic/commercial use cases. Shutterstock's core download revenue is declining, masked by ~$100M in one-off AI data licensing deals (e.g., $250M OpenAI deal through 2027). Once these licensing deals are normalized, the underlying business erosion becomes visible.

Name: WPP PLC
Description: World's largest advertising and marketing services holding company, owning agencies like GroupM, Ogilvy, and AKQA. Provides advertising, media buying, PR, branding, and digital marketing services globally.
Impact: Generative AI can produce ad copy, creative assets, media plans, and marketing content that WPP's agencies currently charge premium rates to create. Clients are already using AI tools to reduce agency scope and headcount needs. WPP's stock has underperformed as investors price in margin compression from AI-driven creative automation. The company is investing heavily in AI tools but faces the fundamental threat that AI makes some agency services less valuable.

Name: Fiverr International Ltd
Description: Online marketplace connecting businesses with freelancers offering digital services including graphic design, copywriting, programming, video editing, and marketing. Platform for gig economy knowledge work.
Impact: AI tools directly replace many of the freelance services sold on Fiverr: AI can now write copy, generate logos, create marketing content, code simple applications, and produce videos. This threatens both the volume of freelance gigs and the pricing power of freelancers. The platform faces compression from both sides—fewer buyers need freelancers for AI-automatable tasks, and freelancers using AI tools face pricing pressure.

Name: ASGN Inc
Description: Provider of IT staffing and professional services including technology consulting, digital transformation, and government IT solutions. Places IT professionals in contract and permanent roles across commercial and government sectors.
Impact: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) reduce demand for contract programmers and IT consultants. Enterprises can accomplish more with fewer developers, reducing the volume of IT staffing engagements. AI-assisted IT operations reduce need for temporary IT support staff. The IT staffing segment is particularly exposed because software development and IT support are among the tasks most enhanced by AI productivity tools.

Name: Coursera Inc
Description: Online learning platform offering courses, certificates, and degree programs from universities and companies worldwide. Provides both consumer and enterprise learning content. ~150M+ registered learners.
Impact: AI chatbots can now provide personalized tutoring, answer questions, and teach concepts interactively—competing with Coursera's pre-recorded lecture model. Free AI tools make much of Coursera's general education content less differentiated. AI also enables competitors to create courses faster and cheaper. While Coursera has integrated AI features, the fundamental value proposition of 'access to expert knowledge' is being commoditized by AI.

Name: Telecom plus PLC
Description: UK multi-utility provider offering bundled gas, electricity, broadband, mobile, and insurance services through a network marketing (partner) model. Trades as Utility Warehouse.
Impact: AI-powered comparison platforms and automated switching services reduce the value of Telecom Plus's human partner sales network. AI chatbots and virtual assistants can handle the customer acquisition and service roles that human partners currently perform. The network marketing model—relying on human relationships for sales—becomes less defensible as AI can match or exceed human product recommendations and customer service.

Name: Kelly Services Inc Class A
Description: Staffing company providing workforce solutions in office, professional, industrial, science, engineering, technology, and education sectors. One of the original temporary staffing agencies, founded 1946.
Impact: Same structural AI headwinds facing the entire staffing industry: AI automates the recruiting/matching process that staffing firms monetize, and AI/automation reduces overall demand for temporary workers. Kelly's mix of office/clerical temp staffing is particularly exposed as AI handles more administrative and data-entry tasks. Science and engineering segments have some insulation but professional staffing broadly faces displacement risk.

Name: Wolters Kluwer NV
Description: Global information services company providing software and expert solutions for healthcare, tax & accounting, financial services, compliance, and legal professionals. Deep domain expertise embedded in workflow tools.
Impact: AI threatens to commoditize the expert knowledge that Wolters Kluwer has historically packaged and sold at premium prices. AI can now perform legal research, tax calculations, regulatory compliance checks, and clinical decision support—core Wolters Kluwer use cases. However, WKL is better positioned than most on this list because its products are deeply embedded in professional workflows and it's actively integrating AI into its own platforms.

Name: H & R Block Inc
Description: Tax preparation company offering assisted and DIY tax filing services through retail offices, online platforms, and software. Serves ~20M tax returns annually. Also offers financial products and small business services.
Impact: AI can now prepare basic to moderately complex tax returns conversationally, threatening both H&R Block's retail tax prep locations (where humans prepare returns) and its DIY software (where AI chatbots provide a more intuitive experience). ChatGPT and similar tools can answer most routine tax questions that drive customers to H&R Block. The IRS's free Direct File program combined with AI assistance further erodes the value proposition.

Name: Adobe Inc
Description: Software giant providing creative tools (Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere Pro), document management (Acrobat), and digital marketing solutions. Dominant position in professional creative workflows.
Impact: AI image and video generation tools (Midjourney, DALL-E, Runway) threaten Adobe's creative software monopoly by enabling non-designers to create professional content. While Adobe has launched Firefly (its own AI generator), the risk is that AI reduces the need for complex creative software altogether—why learn Photoshop when AI generates the image directly? The stock trades at a premium that may not be justified if AI compresses the total addressable market for creative tools.

Name: LegalZoom.com Inc
Description: Online legal technology platform providing business formation, legal document preparation, registered agent services, intellectual property filing, and compliance solutions for small businesses and individuals.
Impact: AI can now draft legal documents, file business formations, answer legal questions, and handle compliance tasks that LegalZoom charges for. ChatGPT and specialized legal AI tools provide much of LegalZoom's core service at near-zero marginal cost. The company's value proposition of 'affordable legal services for small businesses' is undercut when AI makes those same services essentially free.

Name: Genpact Ltd
Description: Global professional services firm providing business process management and IT services, with focus on digital transformation, analytics, and enterprise AI. Serves Fortune 500 companies across finance, insurance, and manufacturing.
Impact: AI automates the business processes (accounting, insurance claims processing, supply chain management) that Genpact's workforce of ~125,000 handles for enterprise clients. The business process outsourcing model is threatened when AI can perform rules-based processing, data entry, and document analysis faster and cheaper than offshore labor. Genpact is pivoting to AI implementation consulting but faces the classic innovator's dilemma.

Name: Cognizant Technology Solutions Corp
Description: Global IT services and consulting company providing digital transformation, technology modernization, and business process services. ~350,000 employees serving clients in banking, healthcare, manufacturing, and retail.
Impact: AI coding tools and agentic AI workflows threaten to reduce the volume of IT services work that clients outsource to firms like Cognizant. AI can generate code, automate testing, handle basic IT operations, and perform data analysis—tasks that make up a significant portion of Cognizant's billable hours. The IT services industry faces a structural shift where clients need fewer human developers and more AI-augmented delivery, compressing margins and headcount.

Name: Omnicom Group Inc
Description: Global advertising and marketing holding company owning agencies including BBDO, DDB, TBWA, and FleishmanHillard. Provides advertising, strategic media planning, CRM, PR, and specialty marketing services. Announced merger with IPG.
Impact: Same dynamics as WPP: generative AI can produce ad creative, media plans, and marketing content faster and cheaper. Clients are exploring AI-first creative workflows that reduce reliance on agency labor. The advertising holding company model faces margin pressure as AI tools enable in-house marketing teams to handle work previously outsourced to agencies. The Omnicom-IPG merger is partly a defensive consolidation against this AI-driven disruption.

Name: Barrett Business Services Inc
Description: Professional employer organization (PEO) providing HR outsourcing, payroll, workers' comp insurance, and business management solutions to small and medium-sized businesses.
Impact: AI-powered HR platforms (Gusto, Rippling, Deel) can automate payroll, benefits administration, compliance, and HR management that BBSI provides through human-intensive service models. Small businesses may increasingly opt for AI-enabled self-service HR tools rather than PEO relationships. However, BBSI's workers' comp insurance and local relationship model provide some insulation from pure AI displacement.

Name: Korn Ferry
Description: Global organizational consulting firm providing executive search, talent acquisition, leadership development, and workforce management advisory. Helps companies hire, develop, and retain talent.
Impact: AI threatens executive search and talent acquisition processes that Korn Ferry charges premium fees for. AI can now source candidates, screen resumes, assess cultural fit, and even conduct preliminary interviews. LinkedIn's AI features and specialized AI recruiting tools reduce the premium value of human headhunters. The consulting/advisory side has more durability, but the transactional search business faces significant AI-driven compression.

Name: Ibex Ltd
Description: Global provider of outsourced customer engagement and technology solutions. Operates contact centers handling customer support, technical support, and sales for brands across telecom, retail, fintech, and healthcare.
Impact: Same structural threat as TTEC and Concentrix: AI chatbots and virtual agents replace human contact center agents. Ibex's core value proposition of providing lower-cost offshore human labor for customer service is directly threatened by AI that can handle routine inquiries 24/7 at near-zero marginal cost. The company is small enough that the AI transition poses an existential risk to its current business model.

Name: Upwork Inc
Description: Global freelancing platform connecting businesses with independent professionals for projects in development, design, writing, marketing, and administrative work. Largest platform for remote freelance work.
Impact: AI tools directly displace freelance work in writing, design, basic coding, data entry, and marketing—the highest-volume categories on Upwork. Businesses can use AI instead of hiring a freelancer for many routine knowledge tasks. Upwork has seen pressure on take rates and gross services volume. Like Fiverr, the platform faces a shrinking middle market where tasks are too simple for human freelancers (AI handles them) but the complex work remains human-intensive.

Name: Relx PLC
Description: Global provider of information-based analytics and decision tools for professional and business customers. Operates Elsevier (scientific publishing), LexisNexis (legal), and risk analytics divisions.
Impact: AI threatens to disintermediate RELX's information aggregation business: researchers can use AI to synthesize literature instead of searching Elsevier journals; lawyers can use AI for legal research instead of LexisNexis; AI can perform risk assessments that RELX's analytics products handle. However, like Wolters Kluwer, RELX's deeply embedded workflow tools and proprietary datasets provide defensive moats. The threat is real but the company has meaningful ability to integrate AI into its own products.

Name: Semrush
Description: Online visibility management SaaS platform providing SEO, PPC, content marketing, social media, and competitive intelligence tools. Serves digital marketers, agencies, and businesses.
Impact: AI-powered search (Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT search) is fundamentally changing SEO, which is SEMrush's core market. If AI answers queries directly and reduces click-through to websites, the entire premise of SEO—optimizing content to rank in search results—becomes less valuable. AI also automates much of the keyword research, content optimization, and competitive analysis that SEMrush's tools facilitate.

Name: News Corp Class A
Description: Global media and publishing company owning The Wall Street Journal, New York Post, HarperCollins Publishers, Dow Jones, REA Group (real estate), and various Australian media outlets.
Impact: AI-generated news summaries reduce referral traffic to news publishers, threatening the advertising and subscription models. Google AI Overviews and ChatGPT answer queries that previously drove users to WSJ and other News Corp properties. HarperCollins faces generative AI disrupting book publishing (AI-authored content, AI-generated summaries reducing book purchases). News Corp has licensing deals with AI companies but the structural traffic erosion persists.

Name: Publicis Groupe SA
Description: Global advertising and communications holding company owning agencies Saatchi & Saatchi, Leo Burnett, and Sapient. Provides advertising, media, digital transformation, and data/technology services.
Impact: Generative AI enables clients to create ad copy, visuals, and media plans with less agency involvement. Like WPP and Omnicom, Publicis faces margin pressure as AI automates creative and media buying functions. Publicis has been somewhat better positioned through its early investment in data (Epsilon acquisition) and AI tools (Marcel platform), but the structural shift away from labor-intensive agency models still poses long-term risk.

Name: Recruit Holdings 5 Unsponsored ADR Representing Ord Shs
Description: Japanese conglomerate and the world's largest HR technology and staffing company. Owns Indeed and Glassdoor (job platforms), plus major staffing operations in Japan, US, and Europe.
Impact: AI threatens both Recruit's staffing operations (same dynamics as ManpowerGroup/Robert Half) and its job platform business (Indeed/Glassdoor). AI can automate job matching, resume screening, and candidate sourcing that Indeed facilitates. AI-driven hiring tools from LinkedIn and startups compete directly with Indeed's model. Longer-term, AI reducing overall hiring volume (fewer humans needed) shrinks the total addressable market for all job platforms.

Name: IPD Group Ltd
Description: Global advertising and marketing services holding company owning agencies including McCann, FCB, MullenLowe, and Weber Shandwick. Being acquired by Omnicom in a mega-merger announced Dec 2024.
Impact: Same dynamics as the other ad holding companies: generative AI automates creative production, media planning, and marketing analytics that agencies bill for. IPG's stock has underperformed as the industry faces structural AI-driven margin compression. The Omnicom-IPG merger is explicitly a defensive consolidation—combining scale to invest in AI tools and offset revenue pressure from clients bringing AI-enabled work in-house.

Name: Endava plc
Description: London-headquartered technology services company providing outsourced software development, digital transformation, and IT consulting. Operates nearshore delivery centers across Eastern Europe (Romania, Moldova, Bulgaria) and Latin America, serving clients in financial services, payments, healthcare, and retail. ~11,000 employees.
Impact: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) directly reduce the volume of outsourced software development work that is Endava's core business. Clients can accomplish more with fewer external developers, shrinking the billable hours and headcount-based engagements that drive revenue. Stock has fallen ~93% from its Nov 2022 level (~$71 to ~$5). Revenue declined 5-9% YoY through FY2026, and the company swung to a loss as it invested heavily in an AI pivot (Dava.Flow methodology) that has yet to offset the erosion of traditional time-and-materials contracts. Management explicitly warns of near-term revenue headwinds from AI-driven automation compressing legacy delivery models.

Name: EPAM Systems Inc
Description: Global leader in digital platform engineering and software development outsourcing. Headquartered in Pennsylvania with ~55,000 engineers across 50+ countries including major delivery centers in Eastern Europe, India, and Latin America. Revenue of ~$5.5B in 2025. Serves Fortune 500 clients across financial services, healthcare, life sciences, and technology sectors.
Impact: The bellwether for AI disruption of the IT services outsourcing industry. AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) enable enterprise clients to accomplish more software development with fewer outsourced engineers, directly threatening the headcount-based billing model. Stock fell 59% from ~$344 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$131 in Feb 2026. Despite beating Q4 2025 earnings estimates, stock crashed 21% in a single day (Feb 20, 2026) after management guided for just 4.5-7.5% revenue growth in 2026—a dramatic deceleration from 15% in 2025. AI-related revenues ($600M projected for 2026) are growing but cannot offset the structural decline in traditional time-and-materials software development contracts. The broader 2026 software selloff has amplified fears that AI will permanently shrink the total addressable market for outsourced IT services.

Name: Expensify Inc
Description: Cloud-based expense management platform for receipt scanning, expense reporting, corporate card management, and travel booking. Primarily serves SMBs. Went public in 2021 at a ~$4B valuation. ~626,000 paid members as of Jan 2026, down from ~684,000 a year prior.
Impact: AI-powered automation from larger competitors (SAP Concur, Brex, Ramp) is eroding Expensify's core value proposition of simplifying expense workflows—tasks that AI now handles natively inside broader finance platforms. Stock fell ~90% from $9.17 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$0.95. Revenue declined 7.6% in FY2024 to $139M and continued falling through 2025. Paid members dropped 6% YoY. The company swung to a $21.4M net loss in FY2025, and 2026 free cash flow guidance was slashed 66%. While Expensify is investing in its own AI agent (Concierge), the competitive moat has narrowed as AI-native fintech tools make standalone expense management less differentiated.

Name: Perion Network Ltd
Description: Israeli digital advertising technology company specializing in search advertising distribution, display/video advertising, and publisher monetization. Built its business on a lucrative partnership with Microsoft Bing's search distribution marketplace, which at peak contributed ~35% of revenue. Revenue hit $743M in 2023 before collapsing. Market cap ~$364M.
Impact: A casualty of Microsoft's AI pivot in search. When Microsoft integrated Copilot into Bing and restructured its search advertising ecosystem for an AI-first world, it fundamentally changed the search distribution marketplace that Perion depended on. In Q1 2024, Bing implemented ad pricing and mechanism changes that reduced Perion's search advertising activity. Then in June 2024, Microsoft excluded Perion and other publishers from the Bing search distribution marketplace entirely. Search revenue from Bing went from ~35% of total revenue to under 5% in H2 2024. The Bing contract was not renewed at year-end 2024. Stock fell 68% from $26.60 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$8.57. Full-year 2024 revenue dropped 33% and the company swung to a net loss. Perion is pivoting to AI-driven advertising solutions (CTV, retail media, DOOH) but the core search monetization business was destroyed by Microsoft's AI transformation of Bing.

Name: LivePerson Inc
Description: Pioneer in enterprise conversational AI and chatbot technology, providing the Conversational Cloud platform that enables businesses to automate customer interactions via messaging, voice, and AI-powered bots. Founded in 1995 and once the dominant enterprise chatbot vendor. Revenue peaked at ~$514M in 2022. Executed a 1:15 reverse stock split in Oct 2025.
Impact: Perhaps the most ironic AI casualty: an AI company destroyed by better AI. Before ChatGPT, LivePerson was the go-to enterprise chatbot platform. After ChatGPT launched, generative AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and others commoditized conversational AI—enterprises could now build superior chatbots using foundation models directly, reducing the need for LivePerson's proprietary platform. Revenue has roughly halved from ~$514M (2022) to a projected ~$240-255M (2025), declining 19-26% YoY across recent quarters. Net retention rate dropped to ~80%, reflecting persistent customer cancellations and downsells. Stock has fallen ~98% on a split-adjusted basis from Nov 29, 2022 (~$10.68 pre-split equivalent) to ~$3.04. The company is attempting to pivot by integrating LLMs into its platform (Bella AI), but faces intense competition from AI-native alternatives and carries a heavy debt load.

Name: Forrester Research Inc
Description: Global research and advisory firm providing proprietary analyst reports, data, consulting, and events to technology and business leaders. Sells premium access to research insights, vendor evaluations (Wave reports), and strategic advisory services. Revenue peaked at ~$530M in 2022 before declining to ~$397M in 2025, with a projected further drop to $345-360M in 2026.
Impact: AI and LLMs are commoditizing the premium market research and advisory services that Forrester sells. ChatGPT and other AI tools can now summarize markets, compare vendors, analyze competitive landscapes, and generate strategic recommendations—core capabilities that Forrester's analyst reports provide. Revenue declined 8% in 2025 to $397M, with consulting down 9% and events down 29%. The company posted a $119M net loss in 2025 (including goodwill impairment), is sunsetting its strategy consulting business entirely, cut 8% of its workforce, and projects a further 9-13% revenue decline in 2026. Stock has fallen 81% from $34.03 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$6.43. As one industry observer noted, AI has destroyed the informational advantage that made premium analyst research valuable—the 'research factory' middle of the analyst market is being automated. While Forrester is launching its own AI product (AI Access), the structural shift from expensive human-curated research to AI-generated insights represents a permanent compression of the market research industry's pricing power.

Name: Ziff Davis Inc
Description: Global digital media company owning technology and lifestyle brands including PCMag, Mashable, Lifehacker, IGN, and Offers.com. Business model heavily reliant on search-driven affiliate commerce—users arrive via Google search, read product reviews, and click affiliate links to purchase. Also operates Ookla (Speedtest) and health/martech divisions. Revenue ~$1.4B in 2025. Announced $1.2B sale of Connectivity division (Ookla) to Accenture in March 2026.
Impact: AI-powered search is destroying Ziff Davis's core revenue engine. Google AI Overviews and ChatGPT now answer product comparison and tech recommendation queries directly, eliminating the high-intent search traffic that drove users to PCMag reviews and affiliate purchase links. The Technology & Shopping segment—the company's largest—declined 18% YoY in Q4 2025 ($108.9M vs $132.9M), and management expects continued double-digit declines in 2026. CEO Vivek Shah acknowledged that AI search traffic loss is essentially irreplaceable: the high-intent consumers who arrived via search to click through and purchase are simply gone. Stock has fallen 52% from $88.36 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$42.41 (and was as low as ~$27 before a temporary bounce from the Ookla asset sale). Q4 2025 earnings missed estimates, triggering a 12% single-day drop. The $1.2B Connectivity sale to Accenture—at a price exceeding the company's entire pre-deal market cap—signals management is liquidating its healthiest non-AI-disrupted assets to return capital, tacitly conceding that the core digital media business faces structural AI headwinds.

Name: Five9 Inc
Description: Cloud contact center software provider offering intelligent virtual agents (IVA), automatic call distribution, IVR, omnichannel routing, workforce management, and analytics for enterprise customer service operations. The leading independent CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) platform with ~3,000 customers. Revenue peaked at ~$1B run rate in 2023. Stock peaked above $200 in 2021.
Impact: AI agents are structurally shrinking Five9's addressable market. The company's core revenue model is per-seat licensing—charging enterprises for each human agent using its contact center software. As AI virtual agents and chatbots replace human contact center staff, the total number of seats enterprises need declines, directly compressing Five9's revenue base. Stock has fallen 71% from $60.23 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$17.71. The stock dropped 50% in 2025 alone and hit a 52-week low of $16.10 in February 2026. Revenue growth decelerated from 30%+ to single digits (~8% in Q4 2025) as enterprises slow contact center hiring in favor of AI alternatives. The company is attempting to pivot to AI agent pricing (~$40-50/month per AI agent) and usage-based billing, but faces a painful cannibalization dynamic: if one AI agent replaces five human seats, Five9 must grow AI revenue 5x faster than seat revenue declines. Enterprise AI bookings doubled YoY and AI ARR reached $100M, but this cannot yet offset the structural erosion of the per-seat model. Competitors like Amazon Connect, Google CCAI, and AI-native startups are also pressuring Five9's position as the contact center shifts from human-operated software to AI-first automation.

Name: TripAdvisor Inc
Description: Global online travel platform and reviews aggregator with over 1 billion reviews and opinions covering hotels, restaurants, experiences, and attractions. Historically the world's largest travel guidance site, built on search-driven traffic from Google. Revenue of ~$1.9B in 2025. Monthly visits dropped from 146-169M in early 2023 to ~120M by early 2025. Cut ~20% of workforce (~450 people) in 2025 restructuring.
Impact: Google AI Overviews and AI-powered travel planning tools are destroying TripAdvisor's core traffic engine. The company's legacy business model depends on users arriving via Google search to read reviews and click through to book hotels—but AI now answers travel queries directly in search results, eliminating the click-through. CEO Matt Goldberg explicitly cited 'ongoing declines in flyby visitors due to the changing search landscape and the rise of AI overviews,' while CMO Matthew Dacey warned that 'Google's AI mode in search is going to eat large chunks of search.' Stock has fallen ~49% from $19.58 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$10.07. The hotel metasearch segment—the legacy core—declined 14% in Q4 2025, with management guiding for continued mid-to-high teens revenue declines in Hotels & Other through 2026. Zero-click searches now account for 69% of all queries, and AI Overviews jumped from 10-15% of search results to nearly 60% in 2025. The company is pivoting to Experiences (Viator) and dining (TheFork), which now comprise two-thirds of revenue, but the original reviews-and-metasearch business that defined TripAdvisor is in structural decline. Activist investors Starboard Value and Palliser Capital are pushing for asset sales or a full company sale, signaling that the standalone public company model may not survive the AI-driven erosion of search-referral travel traffic.

Name: Sprout Social Inc
Description: Cloud-based social media management platform providing publishing, scheduling, analytics, social listening, engagement, and influencer marketing tools for businesses and agencies. Priced at a premium ($249+/month per seat) targeting mid-market and enterprise customers. ~30,000 customers with ~3,800 contributing $30K+ ARR. Revenue of ~$406M in FY2025.
Impact: AI is compressing the value of specialized social media management software from multiple angles. ChatGPT and AI writing tools can generate social posts, captions, and marketing copy that SMBs previously needed Sprout's content tools to create. Cheaper AI-powered competitors (SocialPilot at $30/mo, Buffer at $6/mo) now bundle AI content generation, sentiment analysis, and scheduling at a fraction of Sprout's $249/seat price, driving SMB churn. Dollar-based net retention rate collapsed to 100% in FY2025—meaning expansion revenue was entirely wiped out by downsells and cancellations. Revenue growth decelerated from 31% (2023) to 22% (2024) to 12.7% (2025) with just 7.7% projected for 2026. Stock has fallen 89% from $55.16 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$5.79, and is down 95% from its 2021 peak. The broader SaaS sector rout—driven by fears that AI commoditizes horizontal software—has hit Sprout especially hard because its core functions (content creation, scheduling, analytics, community management) are exactly the tasks generative AI handles well. The company launched 'Sprout AI' and an agentic AI analyst called 'Trellis' in late 2025, but these features haven't stemmed the valuation collapse as investors question whether a $249/seat social media tool can survive when AI makes the underlying tasks near-free.

Name: Clarivate PLC
Description: Global provider of research analytics, intellectual property intelligence, and scientific data services. Owns Web of Science (academic citation database), Derwent (patent analytics), CompuMark (trademark research), and LifeSci/Healthcare data platforms. Serves researchers, universities, law firms, pharma companies, and governments. Revenue of ~$2.46B in 2025. Market cap ~$1.7B, down from ~$12B at peak.
Impact: AI is commoditizing the premium research synthesis, patent analysis, and IP intelligence that Clarivate packages and sells at subscription prices. ChatGPT and AI research tools can now perform literature reviews, summarize scientific papers, analyze patent landscapes, and generate competitive intelligence reports—core use cases for Clarivate's Web of Science and Derwent platforms. Stock has fallen 71% from $9.17 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$2.62. Revenue declined from $2.63B (2023) to $2.56B (2024) to $2.46B (2025), with transactional revenue down 11.9% in Q4 2025 as customers shift to AI-powered alternatives for one-off research tasks. 2026 guidance projects a further decline to $2.30-2.42B with continued net losses ($124-189M). RBC Capital explicitly cited 'ongoing AI risks' when lowering its price target from $5 to $3 in February 2026. The company is attempting to pivot by embedding AI into its own products (Clarivate Nexus AI assistant, Derwent Patent Monitor with AI threat analysis, partnership with Anthropic's Claude), but faces the fundamental challenge that AI makes the act of synthesizing and searching research literature—Clarivate's core value proposition—dramatically cheaper and more accessible outside its walled-garden platforms.

Name: Definitive Healthcare Corp
Description: Provider of healthcare commercial intelligence data and analytics, offering a SaaS platform with detailed profiles on hospitals, physicians, insurance claims patterns, and clinical activity across the US healthcare system. Serves pharma/life sciences companies, health systems, and healthcare IT vendors who use the data for commercial strategy, sales targeting, and market analysis. Revenue peaked at ~$251M in 2023. ~678 employees. Market cap ~$120M, down from ~$2.4B at IPO peak.
Impact: AI is commoditizing the curated healthcare data intelligence that Definitive Healthcare packages and sells at premium subscription prices. AI tools can now synthesize claims data, map physician referral networks, analyze prescribing patterns, and generate commercial insights for pharma sales teams—core use cases for DH's platform. AI-native competitors like Komodo Health are using modern AI architectures and purpose-built healthcare AI engines (Komodo's 'Marmot') to generate patient-level insights that bypass traditional claims data aggregation, directly threatening DH's data moat. Stock has fallen 89% from $10.69 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$1.15. Revenue has declined for two consecutive years—from $251M (2023) to $242M (2024) to $241M (2025)—with 2026 guidance projecting a further 6-9% decline to $220-226M. Net dollar retention has weakened as upsell motions face pressure, and remaining performance obligations dropped 18% YoY as customers shift to shorter one-year deals. Stephens cut its price target from $3 to $2 citing AI-related uncertainty. While management positions AI as a 'tailwind' and plans GenAI features for its Vue platform, the structural reality is that AI makes healthcare data synthesis dramatically cheaper and more accessible outside DH's walled-garden platform—the same dynamic eroding Clarivate and other premium data aggregators.

Name: Freshworks Inc
Description: Cloud-based customer support and IT service management (ITSM) SaaS platform offering helpdesk ticketing, CRM, and IT operations tools for small and mid-market businesses. Products include Freshdesk (customer support), Freshservice (ITSM), and Freshsales (CRM). ~4,500 employees. Revenue of ~$839M in FY2025 with ~$907M ARR. Market cap ~$2.3B, down from ~$12B at IPO peak in 2021.
Impact: A poster child of the February 2026 'SaaSpocalypse'—the $285B+ enterprise software selloff triggered by fears that AI agents will structurally break the per-seat SaaS business model. AI chatbots and autonomous agents can now resolve customer support tickets end-to-end, reducing the number of human agents who need Freshworks' helpdesk software licenses. AI coding tools ('vibe coding') enable businesses to build custom support workflows without buying off-the-shelf SaaS. Jefferies downgraded FRSH to Hold, specifically citing AI disruption risk to the customer experience business. Piper Sandler also downgraded on fears of seat compression as AI replaces human support agents. Stock has fallen 41% from $14.05 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$8.23, and is down 95% from its 2021 IPO peak. Revenue growth has decelerated from 20%+ (2023) to ~14% (2025), with 2026 profit guidance missing estimates. While Freshworks is investing in its own AI agent platform (Freddy AI, achieving 70% ticket deflection across 5,000+ organizations), the fundamental threat is that AI makes the per-seat helpdesk model less valuable—if one AI agent handles the work of five human agents, the total seats needed shrinks dramatically, compressing the revenue base for all customer support SaaS vendors.

Name: TaskUs Inc
Description: Global digital outsourcing company providing content moderation (trust & safety), customer experience, and AI data annotation services to major tech companies. Largest client is Meta (~27% of revenue). Operations across 28 countries with ~47,000 employees. Named a Leader in Everest Group's Data Annotation and Labeling PEAK Matrix. Revenue hit a record $1.18B in 2025. Market cap ~$900M, down from ~$3B at its 2021 peak.
Impact: AI is disrupting TaskUs from multiple directions simultaneously. Its largest client, Meta (27% of revenue), has explicitly signaled it will leverage AI to automate content moderation and reduce outsourced work volumes in 2026—directly contributing to TaskUs's guidance of just 3.5% revenue growth, a dramatic deceleration from 19% in 2025. AI-powered moderation tools can increasingly handle the trust & safety work (flagging harmful content, financial crime compliance) that human moderators perform, and management acknowledged trust & safety will likely have the lowest growth rate in 2026 'given the impact that automation is likely to have on core content moderation volumes.' Stock has fallen 45% from $18.73 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$10.20, and dropped 39% in the six months following record 2025 earnings as investors priced in structural AI headwinds. TaskUs faces a deeper paradox in its AI data annotation business: while AI Services grew 46% in 2025, the improving capability of AI models means they increasingly need less human-labeled training data (via synthetic data, self-supervision, and RLHF improvements), potentially shrinking the very segment that is supposed to offset legacy declines. The company is investing $25M+ in AI transformation and pivoting to outcome-based pricing, but the fundamental tension remains—TaskUs's human-labor-intensive model is being squeezed by the same AI technology it helps train and moderate.

Name: Globant SA
Description: Argentina-founded global digital and cognitive transformation company providing AI-driven software development, digital product engineering, and technology consulting. Operates nearshore delivery centers across Latin America (Argentina, Colombia, Brazil, Mexico, Uruguay) and increasingly in India and Eastern Europe. ~29,000 employees serving enterprise clients in financial services, media, technology, and consumer sectors. Revenue peaked at ~$2.45B in 2025. Market cap ~$1.9B, down from ~$8B at its 2021 peak.
Impact: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) are structurally reducing demand for the outsourced software development that is Globant's core business. Enterprises can accomplish more with fewer external developers, compressing the headcount-based engagements that drive revenue. Stock has fallen 74% from $174.22 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$44.95. Revenue growth collapsed from 20%+ annual rates pre-ChatGPT to essentially flat in 2025 (~1% growth), with Q4 2025 revenue declining 4.7% YoY. The company missed Q4 2025 earnings estimates and guided for just 0.2-2.2% revenue growth in 2026. Globant is attempting to pivot from traditional time-and-materials software delivery to an AI-native 'Pods' model—small teams of engineers augmented by AI agents using a token-based subscription pricing model rather than per-seat billing. AI Pods reached a $20.6M exit-rate ARR in 2025 with 45-60% gross margins, and management targets $60-100M by year-end 2026. However, this nascent AI revenue cannot yet offset the structural erosion of traditional delivery contracts. The broader IT services sector rout—driven by fears that AI permanently shrinks the total addressable market for outsourced software development—has hit Globant especially hard given its premium historical valuation and pure-play exposure to digital engineering work.

Name: Asana Inc
Description: Cloud-based work management and project collaboration platform providing task tracking, project timelines, workflow automation, goals tracking, and team coordination tools. Serves ~150,000 paying customers from SMBs to enterprises including Amazon, Google, and Johnson & Johnson. Founded by Facebook co-founder Dustin Moskovitz. Revenue of ~$723M in FY2026 (ending Jan 2026). Market cap ~$1.5B, down from ~$28B at its 2021 peak.
Impact: AI agents and copilots are structurally undermining the value of standalone project management SaaS. AI can now autonomously create project plans, assign tasks, generate status updates, triage incoming requests, and manage workflows—core functions that justify Asana's per-seat pricing. The per-seat model is under direct threat as AI 'teammates' handle work that previously required human project managers, compressing the number of seats enterprises need. Stock has fallen 60% from $17.21 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$6.86, and is down 92% from its 2021 peak of ~$145. Net retention rate dropped to 95-96%—below 100%, meaning existing customers are actively downsizing—reflecting persistent churn and downgrades across enterprise and mid-market segments. Revenue growth decelerated from 45%+ (FY2023) to ~9% (Q4 FY2026), with Q4 FY2026 revenue of $205.6M missing analyst consensus. Co-founder CEO Dustin Moskovitz stepped down in late 2025. Asana was named alongside Sprout Social and Five9 in Jefferies' 'SaaSpocalypse' report as SaaS companies most exposed to AI-driven seat compression. The company is pivoting aggressively with AI Studio (no-code AI workflow builder) and AI Teammates (autonomous agents), but faces a cannibalization paradox: the better its AI features work, the fewer human seats customers need. Meanwhile, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, and AI-native competitors are embedding project management capabilities directly into productivity suites, threatening Asana's standalone value proposition.

Name: BILL Holdings Inc
Description: Cloud-based financial operations platform providing accounts payable automation, accounts receivable, spend management, and payment processing for small and mid-sized businesses. Processes ~$95B in payment volume quarterly across ~498,500 businesses. Also offers corporate cards and expense management. Revenue of ~$1.5B in FY2025 (+13% YoY), with Q2 FY2026 total revenue of $414.7M (+14% YoY). Market cap ~$4B, down from ~$30B at its 2021 peak. ~2,400 employees.
Impact: AI-native fintech competitors are building from-scratch autonomous AP workflows that structurally undercut BILL's legacy platform. Ramp launched 'Agents for AP'—specialized AI agents for invoice coding, fraud detection, approval routing, and payment optimization—that process invoices with 7x fewer clicks than BILL and close books two days faster. In early access, Ramp's AI agents flagged over $1M in fraudulent invoices in just 90 days. The competitive moat that BILL built over nearly two decades of SMB fintech is eroding as AI collapses the complexity of AP/AR workflows: invoice OCR, GL coding, approval chains, and payment routing are all becoming AI-automated commodities. Stock has fallen 65% from $114.67 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$40.24. Multiple analyst downgrades in early 2026: Morgan Stanley cut from Overweight to Equalweight, KBW lowered its price target to $46, and Truist reduced targets citing lower net customer additions. The broader 2026 SaaS selloff—driven by fears that AI agents will break per-seat and per-transaction pricing models—has hit BILL particularly hard as investors question whether a legacy AP platform can defend against AI-native competitors offering faster, cheaper, and more intelligent financial automation. BILL is deploying its own 'agentic AI' features on its platform, but faces the classic incumbent's dilemma: its revenue model depends on the manual financial workflows that AI is designed to eliminate.

Name: Bumble Inc
Description: Women-first online dating platform operating Bumble (where women make the first move), Badoo (global dating app popular in Europe/Latin America), and Bumble For Friends. ~50 million total users across 150+ countries, though paying users fell 20.5% to 3.3 million by Q4 2025. IPO'd in 2021 at a ~$8.6B valuation. Full-year 2025 revenue of $965.7M, down 9.9% YoY. Absorbed $1.039B in non-cash impairment charges in 2025. Market cap ~$450M, down from ~$14B at its 2021 peak.
Impact: AI is accelerating a multi-front crisis for dating platforms. AI-generated fake profiles now flood dating apps—user reports suggest up to 80% of profiles on major platforms may be fake or AI-generated, up from 10-15% before the 2023 AI boom. Norton blocked 17 million dating scam attacks in Q4 2025 alone (+19% YoY), with AI enabling scammers to generate convincing photos, craft messages that fool 70% of recipients, and operate at unprecedented scale. This erodes the trust and authenticity that dating apps depend on. Simultaneously, AI companion apps (Replika, Character.ai) are competing for the emotional connection users seek, with 25% of young adults believing AI could replace real romantic relationships. 'Swipe fatigue' has intensified as users can no longer distinguish real people from bots. Stock has fallen 84% from $22.40 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$3.60. Paying users declined 11.5% to 3.7M in 2025, with 79% of Gen Z users dropping regular use of dating platforms. Bumble is attempting an AI-powered pivot with 'Bee,' an AI dating concierge that curates matches based on psychology rather than photos, but the company faces a paradox: AI is both the disease destroying user trust and the medicine it's betting on to survive.

Name: Paycom Software Inc
Description: Cloud-based human capital management (HCM) platform providing payroll processing, talent acquisition, time and labor management, HR administration, and benefits enrollment through a single-database architecture. Serves ~37,000 clients, primarily mid-market US employers. Revenue of ~$2.05B in FY2025 (+11% YoY). ~7,000 employees (after 500+ AI-driven layoffs in Oct 2025). Market cap ~$6B, down from ~$22B at its 2021 peak.
Impact: AI is compressing the per-employee SaaS pricing model that underpins Paycom's business from multiple directions. Morningstar explicitly cut its fair value estimate from $208 to $130 and raised its uncertainty rating to Very High, citing 'developing risks posed by AI' and 'a higher likelihood of increased competition and lower seat counts due to clients seeing AI-driven efficiencies.' As AI automates HR administration, payroll processing, benefits enrollment, and employee onboarding, companies need fewer HR staff—directly reducing the per-employee-per-month seats that generate Paycom's revenue. Stock has fallen 60% from $314.73 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$124.82. Revenue growth collapsed from 30% (2021) to 6-7% projected for 2026, with guidance of $2.175-2.195B missing analyst estimates of $2.23B and triggering a 7.9% single-day drop. Paycom itself laid off 500+ employees in October 2025, explicitly citing AI-driven automation—an ironic signal that the same AI efficiencies reducing its own headcount are also shrinking its customers' HR departments. The company faces a compounding 'automation paradox': its flagship Beti product, which empowers employees to process their own payroll, has been so effective at eliminating errors that it cannibalized Paycom's traditional service revenue from fixing those errors. Jefferies cut its price target from $190 to $130. While Paycom is investing in AI features (IWant natural language engine, global HCM expansion to 180+ countries), it faces intensifying competition from AI-native HR platforms and embedded payroll offerings from giants like ADP, Workday, and SAP that are integrating AI capabilities directly into broader enterprise suites.
